name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ ٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَالِصَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘If the abode of the Hereafter were exclusively for you with Allah, and not for other people, then long for death, should you be truthful.’ (94)
```

```arabic
وَلَن يَتَمَنَّوْهُ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٥)
```

```english
But they will not long for it ever because of what their hands have sent ahead, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers. (95)
```

```arabic
وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَوٰةٍ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۚ يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
Surely, you will find them the greediest for life, of all people even the idolaters. Each of them is eager to live a thousand years, though it would not deliver him from the punishment, were he to live [that long]. And Allah watches what they do. (96)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُۥ نَزَّلَهُۥ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٩٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down on your heart with the will of Allah, confirming what has been [revealed] before it and as a guidance and good news for the faithful.’ (97)
```

```arabic
مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّلَّهِ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَجِبْرِيلَ وَمِيكَىٰلَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٨)
```

```english
[Say,] ‘Whoever is an enemy of Allah, His angels and His apostles and Gabriel and Michael, [let him know that] Allah is the enemy of the faithless.’ (98)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
We have certainly sent down manifest signs to you, and no one denies them except transgressors. (99)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** "Say, 'If the abode of the Hereafter...'" etc. Since their saying, "The Fire shall not touch us except for a few numbered days," and their saying, "We believe in what was sent down to us," in response to what was said to them, "Believe in what Allah has sent down," both imply their claim that they alone are saved in the Hereafter to the exclusion of others, and that their salvation and felicity therein are untainted by destruction or misery—because they believe they will not be punished except for a few numbered days, namely the days they worshipped the calf—Allah the Exalted confronted them with a discourse that exposes the falsehood of their claims and shows that they know this without hesitation or doubt. Thus, the Exalted said to His Prophet: **قُلْ إِن كَانَتْ لَكُمُ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ** "Say, 'If the abode of the Hereafter'"—meaning the felicity of that abode, for whoever owns an abode disposes of it as he deems good and pleasing, and resides in its most beautiful and felicitous parts. His saying, the Exalted: **عِندَ اللَّهِ** "with Allah"—meaning established with Him, the Exalted, by His judgment and permission; it is like His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِندَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلَامُ** "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19). His saying, the Exalted: **خَالِصَةً** "exclusively"—meaning untainted by what you dislike of punishment or humiliation, based on your claim that you will not be punished therein except for a few numbered days. His saying, the Exalted: **مِّن دُونِ النَّاسِ** "to the exclusion of [other] people"—this is due to your claim of the invalidity of every religion except yours. His saying, the Exalted: **فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** "then long for death should you be truthful." This is like His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ هَادُوا إِن زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ النَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا الْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** "Say, 'O Jews! If you claim that you are Allah’s favorites, to the exclusion of other people, then long for death, should you be truthful'" (62:6).

This is a holding to account based on an innate necessity (اللازم الفطري) whose effect is manifest in the external world such that not the slightest doubt can occur regarding it. It is that a human being—indeed, every conscious being—when given a choice between comfort and fatigue, chooses comfort without hesitation or vacillation. When given a choice between a life and existence that is murky and tainted and another that is exclusive and pure, he certainly chooses the pure and pleasant one. Even if he were supposedly afflicted with what he inclines away from—such as a miserable and wretched life or a distressed existence—he would never cease longing for the other good and pleasant one; his heart would never be free from yearning for it, his tongue from mentioning it, and his actions from striving toward it.

Therefore, if they were truthful in their claim that the exclusive felicity of the Hereafter belongs to them to the exclusion of other people, it would be mandatory for them to long for it in their hearts (جناناً), with their tongues (لساناً), and with their limbs (أركاناً). But they will never long for it, ever, because of what their hands have sent ahead—such as the killing of prophets, disbelieving in Moses, and breaking covenants. And Allah is All-Knowing of the wrongdoers (الظالمين).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ** "because of what their hands have sent ahead" is a metonymy (كناية) for action (العمل), for most actions, as perceived by the senses, occur by means of the hand, and are then presented to the one who benefits from them or seeks them. There are two subtle points (عنايتان) here: attributing the "sending ahead" to the hands rather than the owners of the hands, and counting every act as a work of the hands.

In short, the actions of a human being—especially those that proceed from him continuously—are the best evidence of what is concealed in his conscience (ضميره) and rooted in his inner self (باطنه). Wicked deeds and foul actions only reveal a foul inner nature (طوية) that refuses to incline toward the meeting with Allah and residing in the abode of His friends.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَتَجِدَنَّهُمْ أَحْرَصَ النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ حَيَاةٍ** "And you will surely find them the greediest of people for life" is like an explanatory proof for His saying, the Exalted: "And they will never long for it, ever." That is, what bears witness to the fact that they will never long for death is that they are the greediest of people for this worldly life; nothing veils or prevents the longing for the abode of the Hereafter except greed for this world and clinging to it. The use of the indefinite form (التنكير) in His saying: **عَلَىٰ حَيَاةٍ** "for a life" is for disparagement (التحقير), as He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known" (29:64).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا** "and [greedier] than the polytheists." It appears that this is coordinated (عطف) with "the people" (الناس), and the meaning is: "And you will surely find them greedier than the polytheists."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا هُوَ بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ مِنَ الْعَذَابِ أَن يُعَمَّرَ** "but it will not distance him from the punishment that he should be granted a [long] life." It appears that **مَا** is negating (نافية). The pronoun **هُوَ** (it) either refers to the matter and the story (للشأن والقصة), in which case **أَن يُعَمَّرَ** (that he be granted life) is the subject (مبتدأ) and its predicate (خبر) is **بِمُزَحْزِحِهِ** (distancing him); or it refers back to what is indicated by His saying: "one of them desires," meaning: "and that which he desires will not distance him from the punishment," and His saying **أَن يُعَمَّرَ** is an explanation (بيان) for it. The meaning of the verse is: They will never long for death, and I swear you will surely find them the greediest of people for this lowly, wretched life that diverts from that felicitous, good life. Rather, you find them greedier for life than the polytheists (الذين أشركوا) who do not believe in a resurrection (بعثاً) or a rising (نشوراً). One of them desires to be granted the longest of lives, yet the longest of lives will not distance him from the punishment, because life—when compared to the Hereafter—is limited and reaches a term and an appointed time (أجل).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوَدُّ أَحَدُهُمْ لَوْ يُعَمَّرُ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ** "Each of them is eager to live a thousand years"—meaning the longest and most extensive life. Thus, "a thousand" is a metonymy for a great number; it is the final rank of numbers in terms of individual word formation (الوضع الافرادي) among the Arabs, and anything beyond it is expressed through repetition and composition, such as ten thousand, a hundred thousand, and a thousand thousand.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ** "and Allah sees best what they do." The All-Seeing (البصير) is one of His Beautiful Names, and its meaning is knowledge of things seen (المبصرات); thus, it is a branch of the name the All-Knowing (العليم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَن كَانَ عَدُوًّا لِّجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ** "Say, 'Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel, then it is he who has brought it down on your heart...'" etc. The context indicates that the verse was revealed as a response to what the Jews said, and that they refused and disdained to believe in what was sent down to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, justifying this by saying they were enemies of Gabriel, who brought the revelation (الوحي) to him. The evidence for this is that Allah, the Glorified, answers them in the Quran regarding both the Quran and Gabriel in the two verses, and the reports concerning the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) support this. He answered their saying, "We do not believe in the Quran because of our enmity toward Gabriel who brought it down," with several points: **First:** Gabriel only brought it down upon your heart by Allah's permission, not from himself; thus, their enmity toward Gabriel should not necessitate their turning away from a speech sent down by Allah's permission. **Second:** The Quran confirms (مصدق) what is in their hands of the true Book, and it makes no sense to believe in one thing while disbelieving in that which confirms it. **Third:** The Quran is a guidance (هدى) for the believers. **Fourth:** It is a glad tiding (بشرى), and how can it be right for a rational person to deviate from guidance and ignore glad tidings, even if the one who brought them were his enemy?

He answered their saying, "We are enemies of Gabriel," by stating that Gabriel is an angel among the angels who has no role except to obey what Allah, the Glorified, commands him, just like Michael and the rest of the angels. They are honored servants who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded. Likewise, the messengers of Allah have no role except through Allah and from Allah, the Glorified. Thus, hating them and treating them as enemies is hating and treating Allah as an enemy. Whoever is an enemy to Allah, His angels, His messengers, Gabriel, and Michael—then indeed Allah is an enemy to them. The two verses point to these two answers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ** "then it is he who has brought it down on your heart." There is a shift (إلتفات) here from the first person to the second person address. It would have been expected to say "on my heart," but it was changed to the second person address to indicate that just as Gabriel has no independent role in sending it down—being merely a commanded and obedient servant—likewise the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم has no independent role in receiving and conveying it, except that his heart is a vessel for the revelation (الوحي) over which he has no personal control, and he is commanded to convey it.

Know that these verses contain, in their concluding parts, various types of shifts (إلتفات), even though the basis in them is the address to the Children of Israel. However, when the address is one of blame and reproach and the speech becomes lengthy, the situation becomes one of weariness (إستملال) in speaking with the addressee and a belittling of his status. It is then appropriate for the eloquent speaker to turn away from the direct address time after time through shift after shift, to indicate that He is not pleased with addressing them due to their poor listening and the baseness of their souls, yet He is not pleased with leaving the address entirely, so as to manifest the right of judgment against them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَدُوٌّ لِّلْكَافِرِينَ** "an enemy to the faithless." Here, a manifest noun (الظاهر) is placed in the position of a pronoun (المضمر). The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to indicate the cause of the judgment (علة الحكم), as if it were said: "For Allah is an enemy to them because they are disbelievers, and Allah is an enemy to the disbelievers."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا يَكْفُرُ بِهَا إِلَّا الْفَاسِقُونَ** "and none defy them except the transgressors." This indicates the cause of disbelief (الكفر), which is transgression (الفسق); thus, they are transgressors because of their disbelief. It is not unlikely that the definite article (اللام) in His saying: **الْفَاسِقُونَ** "the transgressors" refers to a previous mention (للعهد الذكري), pointing to what preceded in the beginning of the Surah in His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ الَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مِيثَاقِهِ** "and He misleads by it none but the transgressors—those who break the covenant of Allah after its pledge" (2:26-27).

As for the discussion regarding Gabriel and the manner of his bringing down the Quran upon the heart of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, as well as the discussion regarding Michael and the angels, it will come in its appropriate place, Allah willing.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Say, 'Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel...'" the two verses, Ibn Abbas said: The reason for the revelation of the verse was what is narrated: When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم arrived in Medina, Ibn Suriya and a group of Jews from the people of Fadak came and asked him, saying: "O Muhammad, how is your sleep? For we have been informed about the sleep of the Prophet who comes at the end of time."

He said: **تنام عيناي وقلبي يقظان** — "My eyes sleep, but my heart is awake." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Now tell us about the child: is it from the man or the woman?" He said: "As for the bones, nerves, and veins, they are from the man; as for the flesh, blood, nails, and hair, they are from the woman." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Why is it that a child resembles his paternal uncles and has nothing of the resemblance of his maternal uncles? Or resembles his maternal uncles and has nothing of the resemblance of his paternal uncles?" He said: "Whichever of the two fluids prevails, the resemblance belongs to it." They said: "You have spoken the truth, O Muhammad. Now tell us about your Lord, what is He?" So Allah, the Glorified, sent down: **قُلْ هُوَ اللَّهُ أَحَدٌ** "Say, 'He is Allah, the One'" to the end of the Surah. Then Ibn Suriya said to him: "One trait remains; if you tell it, I will believe in you and follow you. Which angel comes to you with what Allah sends down to you?" He said: "Gabriel." He said: "That is our enemy; he brings fighting, hardship, and war. Michael brings ease and prosperity. If it were Michael who came to you, we would have believed in you."

**I say:** His saying: **تنام عيناي وقلبي يقظان** — "My eyes sleep, but my heart is awake" has been narrated extensively (استفاض) by both the general (*Sunni*) and the specific (*Shi'i*) sources—that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم would have his eyes sleep while his heart did not sleep. Its meaning is that he was not heedless of himself during sleep; while in sleep, he knew that he was sleeping and that what he was seeing was a vision (رؤيا) he was seeing, not wakefulness. This is something that occasionally happens to the righteous when their souls are pure and occupied with the remembrance of their Lord's station. This is because the soul's oversight of its Lord's station does not leave it heedless of its mode of worldly life and the nature of its attachment to its Lord. This is a type of witnessing (مشاهدة) that clarifies to a human being that he is in the world of this life in a state identical to sleep—both the sleep that people see as only sleep, and the wakefulness that people see as wakefulness—and that people, while they are secluded at the door of the senses and clinging to the earth of nature, are asleep even if they consider themselves awake. It is narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام: **الناس نيام فإذا ماتوا إنتبهوا** — "People are asleep; when they die, they wake up." A more complete treatment of this discussion, as well as the discussion of the other segments of this hadith, will come in appropriate places in this book, Allah willing.
